pmos

Accounting Basics for PMs — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Accounting is the bookkeeping machinery that turns a company's day-to-day transactions into the three financial statements every PM eventually has to read. This primer teaches the mechanics — not valuation, not metrics, but how the books actually work. It covers what accounting is and how it differs from bookkeeping; the accounting equation (Assets = Liabilities + Equity) that every entry must keep in balance; double-entry bookkeeping with debits and credits; the difference between cash-basis and accrual-basis accounting; the revenue recognition principle and the five-step rules behind it; the matching principle; adjusting entries; the eight-step accounting cycle that runs every period; how the income statement and balance sheet are built from the ledger; how the cash flow statement reconciles profit back to cash; the rulebook (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) that governs it all; and what a product manager actually needs from this. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the accounting canon — Corporate Finance Institute, AccountingCoach, the Wikipedia accounting corpus, FreshBooks, NerdWallet, and Baremetrics. 15 verified sources.

What accounting actually is

Accounting is the system a business uses to record, classify, and report what happens to its money. People often use "bookkeeping" and "accounting" as if they mean the same thing, but they sit at different ends of one process. Bookkeeping is the recording: capturing each transaction as it happens. Accounting is the wider craft that includes bookkeeping but also adds the rules, adjustments, and reports that turn raw records into financial statements someone can actually read.

The whole discipline rests on a single recording method called double-entry bookkeeping. Wikipedia describes it as a system in which "every financial transaction is recorded with equal and opposite entries (debits and credits)", so that the books always stay in balance. This is not a modern invention. The Franciscan friar Luca Pacioli, who "first codified the system in his mathematics textbook... published in Venice in 1494", is often called the father of accounting — though merchant ledgers using the method survive from Florence as early as 1299.

For a product manager, the useful framing is this: accounting is the agreed grammar that lets a company describe its own activity in numbers. You do not need to keep the books. You do need to understand the grammar well enough to read what the books say — and to know which parts are hard fact and which parts rest on judgment. Everything in this primer is that grammar, built up one rule at a time.

The accounting equation

The foundation of all accounting is one short equation: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. FreshBooks states it directly and adds the rule that gives it force: "Both sides of this equation must be the same. If they're not, there's a mistake in the books." Read in plain English, it says: everything a company owns (its assets) was paid for either with money it owes to others (its liabilities) or with money the owners put in or earned (its equity).

The three words are worth defining. Assets are the things the business owns or controls that have value — cash, inventory, equipment, money customers owe it. Liabilities are what the business owes to others — loans, unpaid bills, wages not yet paid. Equity is what is left for the owners after the liabilities are subtracted from the assets; it is the owners' stake.

This equation is not just a definition; it is a constant constraint. Every single transaction a business records must leave the equation balanced. If a company borrows money, its cash (an asset) goes up and its loans (a liability) go up by the same amount, so both sides move together. That constraint is what the next concept — double-entry — exists to enforce.

Double-entry, debits, and credits

Double-entry bookkeeping is the rule that every transaction touches at least two accounts, and that the two sides always match. FreshBooks defines it as a method "where each transaction is recorded in 2 or more accounts using debits and credits". An account is simply a record of one kind of thing — a Cash account, a Sales account, a Rent account, and so on.

The words "debit" and "credit" cause more confusion than anything else in accounting, because in plain speech they sound like "subtract" and "add." In accounting they mean something more neutral. AccountingCoach defines them by position: "To debit an account means to enter an amount on the left side of the account. To credit an account means to enter an amount on the right side." A debit is just the left column; a credit is just the right column. Neither is inherently good or bad.

Whether a debit raises or lowers an account depends on the account type. Wikipedia gives the fixed rule: "Debits increase the value of assets and expense accounts," while "credits increase the value of liability, equity, and revenue accounts." AccountingCoach teaches the same thing with two memory aids: DEAL (Dividends or Draws, Expenses, Assets, Losses) for the accounts "increased with a debit", and GIRLS (Gains, Income, Revenues, Liabilities, Stockholders' Equity) for those increased with a credit.

The discipline that makes the system reliable is the balancing rule. For every transaction, AccountingCoach notes, "The total of the amount(s) entered as debits must equal the total of the amount(s) entered as credits." Wikipedia states the same constraint across the whole ledger: "the total debits must be equal to the total credits and therefore balance." This is double-entry's built-in error check — if the two sides do not match, something was recorded wrong. You do not need to make journal entries as a PM, but knowing that every number on a statement came from this debit-equals-credit machinery tells you why the statements can be trusted to internally add up.

Cash basis versus accrual basis

One of the most consequential choices in accounting is when to record a transaction — and there are two answers. NerdWallet draws the line cleanly. Under cash-basis accounting, "income and expenses are recognized only when payments are made." Under accrual-basis accounting, the system "recognizes income and expenses when they are incurred." "Incurred" means when the work is actually done or the obligation actually arises, regardless of when money moves.

The Wikipedia entry on accrual puts the difference in one sentence: accrual accounting recognizes "revenues and expenses when they are earned or incurred, not necessarily when cash is received or paid," which "contrasts with cash basis accounting, which recognizes transactions only when cash changes hands." The concrete example it gives: "if a company delivers a product in one financial year but will receive payment in the next, the revenue is recognized in the current financial year."

This matters enormously for software businesses. A company that sells software as a service — SaaS, meaning the customer pays a subscription to use software hosted by the vendor — often collects a full year of payment up front but delivers the service month by month. Cash basis would book all that money the day it arrives; accrual basis spreads it across the year as the service is actually delivered. NerdWallet notes that accrual "gives a fuller, more accurate view of your business's finances," while "cash basis accounting is not GAAP compliant" — that is, it does not meet the Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, the rulebook covered later — because it "disregards the matching and revenue recognition principles of accounting." Those two principles are the next two sections, and accrual accounting is what makes them possible.

Revenue recognition

Revenue recognition is the rule for when a company is allowed to count a sale as revenue. The intuitive answer — "when we get paid" — is wrong under accrual accounting. Wikipedia states the principle: "revenues are earned and recognized when they are realized or realizable, no matter when cash is received." Baremetrics says the same thing from the company's side: "a company should recognize revenue in the period in which it was earned, and not necessarily when the cash was received."

Because "earned" can be fuzzy, the accounting world standardized it. The governing standard is known as ASC 606 (Accounting Standards Codification Topic 606) in the United States, and as IFRS 15 (International Financial Reporting Standard 15) internationally. Wikipedia summarizes the five-step model it introduced in 2014: identify the contract, "identify the performance obligations" in that contract, "determine the transaction price," "allocate the transaction price" across those obligations, and finally "recognize revenue" when control of the good or service transfers to the customer.

A "performance obligation" is just a distinct promise inside a contract — for example, deliver the software, plus provide a year of support, plus run a one-time setup. Each promise can earn its revenue on its own schedule. Wikipedia notes the timing consequence: because "cash can be received in an earlier or later period than when obligations are met," revenue is sometimes accrued (counted before cash arrives) and sometimes deferred (cash held back as a liability until the promise is delivered). For a PM, this is why a feature that lets customers prepay annually does not instantly become "revenue this quarter" — the finance team will recognize it over the period the product is actually delivered.

The matching principle

The matching principle is revenue recognition's twin, applied to expenses. Where revenue recognition decides when income is counted, the matching principle decides when the costs of earning that income are counted. Baremetrics defines it as the rule that "expenses should be matched to the revenue they generate and therefore be shifted into the period in which the revenue was earned instead of being recorded in the period they were paid for."

An example makes it concrete. Suppose a company pays its sales commissions in January for deals that will deliver value across the whole year. Cash basis would dump the entire commission cost into January. The matching principle instead spreads that cost across the months the related revenue is earned, so each month's profit reflects both the income it produced and the costs that produced it. The result is a truer picture of how profitable each period really was.

This principle is the reason accrual accounting and cash accounting can show wildly different profits for the same business in the same month. It is also the reason the books need adjusting entries — covered next — because reality rarely lines up neatly with calendar months, and someone has to do the shifting. The US GAAP summary on Wikipedia lists matching among the core assumptions, stating that "expenses have to be matched with revenues as long as it is reasonable to do so." The phrase "as long as it is reasonable" is a quiet admission that some of this is judgment, not pure arithmetic.

Adjusting entries

Adjusting entries are the corrections made at the end of each period to make the books obey the accrual, revenue-recognition, and matching rules. AccountingCoach defines them as "accounting journal entries that convert a company's accounting records to the accrual basis of accounting," typically made "just prior to issuing a company's financial statements." They are the bridge between what was literally recorded during the period and what should be reported.

AccountingCoach explains they are needed in two situations. In the first, "Nothing has been entered in the accounting records for certain expenses or revenues, but those expenses and/or revenues did occur" — for instance, interest that has built up but has not been billed yet. In the second, "Something has already been entered in the accounting records, but the amount needs to be divided up between two or more accounting periods" — for instance, a year of insurance paid up front that should be spread over twelve months.

The four common types follow from those two situations: accrued expenses (costs incurred but not yet paid), accrued revenues (income earned but not yet received), deferred or prepaid expenses (payments made in advance and spread out over time), and deferred revenues (money collected before the work is done). Depreciation — spreading the cost of a long-lived asset like a server over its useful life — is a familiar example of the spreading kind. The reason a PM should care: adjusting entries are where judgment enters the numbers most visibly, and they are why the same underlying activity can be reported slightly differently by two equally honest companies.

The accounting cycle

All of the rules so far run on a repeating schedule called the accounting cycle — the sequence the books go through every period to turn transactions into statements. Corporate Finance Institute lays out eight steps. It begins with financial transactions, then journal entries recorded "in chronological order," then posting those entries to the general ledger, which gathers every transaction into its proper account.

After posting comes the trial balance, where, per CFI, "a total balance is calculated for the accounts" to check that debits and credits still match. Wikipedia describes the trial balance as "an internal financial statement that lists the adjusted closing balances of all the general ledger accounts," whose purpose "is to ensure the accuracy of an entity's double-entry bookkeeping system." If the trial balance does not balance, a worksheet step hunts for the error, then adjusting entries are "posted to accounts for accruals and deferrals."

Only then are the statements built. CFI notes that at this point "the balance sheet, income statement, and cash flow statement can be prepared." Wikipedia confirms the trial balance feeds them: once it balances, "the trading profit and loss statement and balance sheet and other financial reports can then be produced." The cycle ends with closing entries, where CFI says "the revenue and expense accounts are closed and zeroed out for the next accounting cycle" so the next period starts fresh. Then the whole loop runs again.

Building the income statement and balance sheet

The two best-known statements both come out of the same ledger, just sliced differently. AccountingCoach makes the shared origin explicit: "the amounts reported on the financial statements originated from the corporation's business transactions that were recorded and stored in the general ledger accounts." The statements are not separate books; they are two views of one set of records.

The income statement reports performance over a stretch of time. AccountingCoach says it shows "revenues, gains, expenses, losses, net income and other totals for the period of time shown in the heading." It is sometimes called the profit-and-loss statement, or P&L (profit and loss). It is built from the revenue and expense accounts — the temporary accounts that the closing step zeroes out — so each one covers exactly one period and starts the next period empty.

The balance sheet reports position at a single instant. It is built from the permanent accounts and is organized around the accounting equation, which AccountingCoach writes as "Assets = Liabilities + Stockholders' Equity." Because these accounts are permanent, AccountingCoach notes that "the account balances at the final instant of an accounting year will carry forward to become the beginning balances of the subsequent year." The clean mental model: the income statement is a video of the period, the balance sheet is a photo at period-end, and both are developed from the same ledger negatives.

The cash flow statement

The third statement exists because accrual accounting, for all its accuracy, can hide whether a company actually has money. Profit measured under the matching and revenue-recognition rules is not the same as cash in the bank. The cash flow statement closes that gap by tracking actual cash, sorted into operating, investing, and financing activities. AccountingCoach describes it as reporting "the sources and uses of cash by operating activities, investing activities, financing activities, and certain supplemental information."

Most companies build the operating section using the indirect method. Corporate Finance Institute explains that this method "starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items (like depreciation) and changes in working capital." In other words, it takes the profit figure from the income statement and walks it back to cash. The reconciling formula CFI gives is "Net Income + Non-Cash Expenses – Increase in Current Assets + Increase in Current Liabilities = Cash Flow from Operations."

The reason for the add-backs is that some expenses reduce profit without ever touching cash. AccountingCoach gives the classic case: depreciation expense "did not use cash. (It was merely an adjusting entry that debited Depreciation Expense and credited Accumulated Depreciation.)" CFI summarizes the whole point: the indirect method "reconciles accrual-based net income to actual cash flow" by adding back non-cash expenses and adjusting for shifts in accounts receivable (AR, money owed by customers), inventory, and accounts payable (AP, money owed to suppliers). For a PM, the takeaway is blunt: a product line can look profitable on the income statement and still drain cash if customers pay slowly — and the cash flow statement is where that shows up.

GAAP and the rulebook

If every company invented its own definitions of "revenue" and "expense," no two financial statements could be compared. The rulebook that prevents this is GAAP — Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. Corporate Finance Institute defines it as "a commonly recognized set of rules and procedures designed to govern corporate accounting and financial reporting in the United States." The standards are set by the Financial Accounting Standards Board, abbreviated FASB; CFI notes they "were developed jointly by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB)."

The purpose is comparability. CFI explains that GAAP's consistency "makes it easy for investors and other interested parties (such as a board of directors) to more easily comprehend financial statements and compare the financial statements of one company with those of another company." The rules were not academic in origin; CFI notes they emerged "primarily as a response to the Stock Market Crash of 1929" and the loss of trust in corporate reporting that followed.

Wikipedia's US GAAP summary lists the core assumptions that everything in this primer has been building toward. There is going concern — the assumption that "the company will continue its business indefinitely." There is revenue recognition: "Companies should record revenue when earned and not when received." There is the matching principle, and there is consistency, so that accounting "remains consistent and comparable across periods." Publicly traded companies in the US must follow GAAP because the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC, the federal markets regulator) requires it. Internationally, many countries instead use IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards); the two overlap heavily but differ in details, which is worth remembering when comparing a US company to a foreign one.

How PMs should read the books

A product manager almost never makes a journal entry. So the practical question is not "how do I keep books?" but "what should I take from them?" The first thing to internalize is that the statements are partly judgment, not pure fact. The adjusting entries, the depreciation schedules, the choices about when an obligation is "satisfied" — these involve estimates. The US GAAP matching rule itself is hedged: expenses are matched to revenue "as long as it is reasonable to do so." Read statements knowing that two honest teams could produce slightly different numbers from the same activity.

The second thing is to separate profit from cash. The income statement answers "did we make money on paper this period?" under accrual rules; the cash flow statement answers "did money actually come in?" A profitable product that bills customers on long payment terms can still starve the company of cash, which is exactly why CFI's indirect method exists to "reconcile accrual-based net income to actual cash flow." When you propose a pricing or billing change, you are moving numbers on both statements, and finance will care about both.

The third thing is to know what each statement can and cannot tell you. The income statement shows a period's performance; the balance sheet shows a moment's position; the cash flow statement shows the movement of cash between them. When a feature affects revenue timing — annual prepay, usage-based billing, free trials — the revenue-recognition rules decide when it lands, not when the customer's card is charged. Speaking in those terms, rather than in raw bookings, is what makes a PM credible in a budget conversation. You do not need to be an accountant; you need to know enough of the grammar to ask the right question and understand the answer.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Accounting mechanics are the base layer that several richer topics build on. The pointers below are the natural next steps once the bookkeeping itself makes sense. None of them are taught in this primer; they are signposts to where the threads lead, so you know what to study next and why it depends on what you have just learned.

  • Financial statement analysis and margins — once you can read the three statements, the next step is interpreting them: gross, operating, and net margins, and what they reveal about a business model. The mechanics here are the prerequisite for that reading.
  • Unit economics and SaaS metrics — revenue recognition and deferred revenue feed directly into subscription metrics like recurring revenue, churn, and customer lifetime value, which restate accounting data in product terms.
  • Working capital and the cash conversion cycle — accounts receivable, inventory, and accounts payable, which the indirect cash flow method adjusts for, are the moving parts of how quickly a business turns activity into cash.
  • Budgeting and forecasting — the accounting cycle produces the historical numbers that planning and forecasting extrapolate from; understanding how the actuals are built makes the forecasts legible.
  • Audit and controls — the double-entry balancing rule and the trial balance are the first line of defense against error and fraud; formal auditing is the discipline that pressure-tests them.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_accounting-basics-for-pms.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
